videos.title: Why Is "11x + 2y" A Sudoku Secret?!

rules.description: White dots mark consecutive digits; black dots mark digits in a 1:2 ratio.
Digits along arrows sum to the digit(s) in the circle or pill.
Digits in dashed cages sum to the given total and don't repeat within the cage.
Adjacent digits on the green line must differ by at least 5.
Digits on the line form a set of consecutive digits in any order.
Digits on lines sum to the same total in each 3×3 box they pass through.
Adjacent digits on the line must differ by at least 4.
British-style crossword where each clue is a wordplay puzzle.
Digits on the line read the same forwards and backwards.
Adjacent cells marked X sum to 10; cells marked V sum to 5.
The solver must deduce the region boundaries as part of solving the puzzle.
No digit may repeat at a chess knight's move distance.
The first X digits in a row/column sum to the clue, where X is the first digit seen.
Cells are hidden until revealed by placing correct digits nearby.
Digits along a parity line must strictly alternate between odd and even.
Marked pairs of adjacent cells must have a 1:2 ratio between them.
Digits must strictly increase from the bulb end to the flat end.
The main diagonals each contain the digits 1–9 exactly once.
Any 3 consecutive cells on a line contain a low (1–3), mid (4–6), and high (7–9) digit.
Multiple groups or regions must have the same total sum.
Digits sandwiched between 1 and 9 in each row/column sum to the clue.
Digits on a line must be strictly between the values of the circles at each end.
Orthogonally adjacent cells may not contain consecutive digits.
No digit may repeat at a chess king's move distance.
Highlighted cells must contain odd or even digits as indicated.
Shade cells so shaded and unshaded cells each form a single connected group, with no 2×2 area fully shaded or unshaded.
Inequality signs between adjacent cells indicate which cell contains the larger digit.
A puzzle whose completed solution yields a hidden meta-answer.
Digits along indicated diagonals sum to the clue (repeats allowed).
Clues indicate how many 'buildings' (taller digits) are visible from that side.
Digits equidistant from the centre of a line sum to the same value.
Any 3 consecutive cells on a line contain digits from each of the three mod-3 classes.
Shaded regions contain identical digit patterns.
No digit on a line appears within that digit's value of itself on the same line.
Additional highlighted regions must also contain 1–9.
Digits in the same position within each 3×3 box must all be different.
The grid uses irregular jigsaw-shaped regions instead of standard 3×3 boxes.
Combines anti-knight, anti-king, and nonconsecutive constraints.
A clue in the corner of four cells lists digits that must appear among those four cells.
Shade cells to form a one-cell-wide snake (connected path) satisfying given constraints.
Marked pairs of adjacent cells must contain consecutive digits.
Digits on a line form a set of consecutive digits in any order (a sequence line).
A digit in a cell indicates the column (or row) in its row (or column) where a specific digit appears.
Exactly one clue in a given set is false; the rest are true.
Shaded cells must contain a digit greater than all orthogonally adjacent unshaded cells.
Four 3×3 windows at fixed positions act as extra boxes, each containing 1–9.
The grid wraps around — the top connects to the bottom and left connects to the right.
Adjacent digits on a line must differ by a minimum value (variant not otherwise specified).
Place stars in regions so each row, column, and region has exactly the same number of stars, with no two stars touching.
Shade cells to form rivers (connected black cells) while islands (white regions) match the given clue numbers.
Draw a single closed loop along grid edges so each numbered cell has that many loop segments around it.
Divide the grid into polyominoes where each cell's number equals the size of its polyomino.
Logic puzzle involving the 12 distinct shapes made of 5 connected squares.
Fill cells to reveal a picture using row/column clues indicating run lengths.
Draw a loop that passes through all circles: straight through white circles (turning before/after), turning at black circles (straight before and after).
Fill each region with digits 1–N (where N is the region size); no two touching cells may share a digit.
Shade cells to form a single connected wall; clues indicate the lengths of shaded runs in the surrounding 8 cells.
Black out cells so no digit repeats in any row or column, no two blacked-out cells touch, and all remaining cells are connected.
Divide the grid into 180°-rotationally-symmetric regions, each containing exactly one circle at its centre of symmetry.
Clues outside the grid indicate the sums of runs of shaded cells in each row and column.
Shade cells so that the unshaded region forms a single connected cave; clues indicate visibility from that cell.
Fill the grid with digits 1–9 so each run sums to its clue with no repeated digits.
Draw a single closed loop through the grid satisfying given constraints.
Locate hidden mines using numeric clues that indicate how many mines are adjacent to each revealed cell.
A logic/pencil puzzle not covered by other rule types.
